era_context: The early medieval period in Europe; the Abbasid Caliphate at its height under Harun al-Rashid (r. 786–809); Tang Dynasty in China under Dezong (r. 779–805); the Byzantine Empire under Constantine VI and his mother Irene as regent; Scandinavian Viking expansion beginning to accelerate.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records for this specific year)

events.title: Death of Empress Dowager Wu (Tang Dynasty)
events.description: Wu Zetian, the only woman to rule China as emperor in her own right (r. 690–705), died in 705 CE. However, her influence on court politics and succession continued to shape Tang governance through her descendants and political legacy. By 780, the reverberations of her reign and the consolidation of power under Dezong reflected the institutional changes she had initiated. This year marks a consolidation point in Tang administrative stability following decades of succession disputes.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Tang court records
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The year 780 itself has no specific documented event; this entry contextualizes the era. Dating is proleptic Gregorian conversion from Chinese lunar calendar.

events.title: Harun al-Rashid becomes Caliph (Abbasid Caliphate)
events.description: Harun al-Rashid ascended to the Abbasid caliphate in 786 CE (not 780), but 780 falls within the reign of his father al-Mansur (r. 754–775) and his brother al-Hadi (r. 775–785). The caliphate in 780 was consolidating power after the civil war of succession (fitna) that followed al-Mansur's death. This period saw the establishment of the Barmakid family as viziers, who would become central to Harun's later administration. The year 780 represents a stable interregnum before the major administrative reforms of Harun's reign.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-l-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: 780 CE is a conventional date; the major succession occurred in 775 and 786. This entry marks the era context rather than a specific event in 780.

events.title: Byzantine Iconoclasm Controversy: Council of Nicaea II preparations
events.description: The Second Council of Nicaea was convened in 787 CE to resolve the iconoclasm controversy (the dispute over the veneration of religious images). In 780, Empress Irene, acting as regent for her young son Constantine VI, was laying groundwork for the council by building consensus among bishops and the imperial court. The iconoclastic period (726–843) had created deep theological and political divisions. Irene's decision to reverse iconoclasm represented a major shift in imperial religious policy and would have profound consequences for Byzantine-Western relations.

sources.name: Acts of the Second Council of Nicaea (787 CE)

events.certainty_note: The council occurred in 787; 780 marks the beginning of Irene's regency and the policy shift. Dating is secure from Byzantine sources.

events.title: Viking raids on Lindisfarne and English coast (ongoing)
events.description: The famous Viking raid on Lindisfarne monastery occurred in 793 CE, not 780. However, 780 falls within the period of increasing Scandinavian maritime activity and raiding in the North Sea. Archaeological and textual evidence suggests that Viking incursions into English and Frankish waters were escalating in the 770s–780s, though major documented raids begin in the 790s. The year 780 represents the threshold of the Viking Age proper, with Norse seafaring technology and navigation improving and raiding becoming more organized.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Scandinavian ship burials and settlement sites

events.certainty_note: No specific raid is documented for 780; this entry contextualizes the era. The Viking Age is conventionally dated from 793 (Lindisfarne), but maritime activity was increasing before this date.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 780 CE has no major documented events in surviving sources. The entries above are contextual approximations. Harun al-Rashid did not become caliph until 786, not 780. The Second Council of Nicaea occurred in 787, not 780. The Lindisfarne raid occurred in 793, not 780. Modern scholarship has moved away from treating 780 as a significant year in any major civilization's history; it is a conventional marker within longer processes.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 780 CE is sparse and indirect. The year falls between major events in multiple civilizations. Byzantine sources (Theophanes) are the most detailed for this period, but they focus on court politics rather than the year 780 specifically. Chinese sources (Shiji, Zizhi Tongjian) are comprehensive but compiled centuries later. Islamic sources (al-Tabari) are similarly retrospective. The lack of contemporary documentation for 780 specifically reflects the nature of medieval record-keeping: events were recorded, but not necessarily dated to the year. Western European sources are thin for this period outside the Frankish realm. The historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and religious events; economic, demographic, and technological changes are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Irene's regency and policy shift in 780 directly led to the convening of the Second Council of Nicaea in 787
The caliphate under al-Hadi in 780 transitioned to Harun al-Rashid's reign in 786, marking a major administrative shift
